parameters.name: Mars mean solar irradiance at top of atmosphere (TOA, normal incidence)
parameters.source: Computed from TSI 1361 W/m2 at 1.5236915 AU; Appelbaum & Flood NASA TM-102299 use 590 (S=1371). Range = aphelion..perihelion.
parameters.notes: Use 590 W/m2 when implementing the A&F lookup tables for self-consistency; difference is 0.7%.

parameters.name: TOA beam irradiance at perihelion (Ls~251 deg)
parameters.notes: Perihelion coincides with southern summer and the dust storm season.

parameters.name: TOA beam irradiance at aphelion (Ls~71 deg)
parameters.source: NASA TM-102299 eq. (4)
parameters.notes: Peri/aphelion flux ratio 1.45 - dominant deterministic seasonal driver.

parameters.name: Mars orbital eccentricity
parameters.source: NASA TM-102299 nomenclature (e = 0.093377); NSSDC Mars Fact Sheet 0.0935

parameters.name: Areocentric longitude of perihelion
parameters.source: Modern ephemeris/NSSDC Mars Fact Sheet (251 deg); A&F TM-102299 used 248 deg
parameters.notes: Use 251 in new code; <1% insolation effect vs 248.

parameters.name: Mars obliquity (axial tilt) for solar declination
parameters.source: NSSDC Mars Fact Sheet; A&F TM-102299 eq. (7) used 24.936 deg. sin(dec) = sin(obliquity) sin(Ls)

parameters.name: Visible dust optical depth, clear (aphelion) season, low latitudes
parameters.source: MER 5-Mars-year record (Lemmon et al. 2015, NTRS 20150008268): Spirit <0.3, Opportunity <0.5 in aphelion season; Viking 'typically about 0.5' (TM-102299); ICES-2019-181 (NTRS 20190027303) non-storm background tau ~0.4
parameters.notes: 880 nm solar-imaging standard; minima ~0.2 observed. verified correction: prior source string misquoted ICES-2019-181 as 'planetary background ~0.48'; that paper states ~0.4. Value 0.45 (range 0.3-0.6) is unchanged and remains defensible.

parameters.name: Dust optical depth, dusty (perihelion) season background (no global storm)
parameters.source: MER/MSL/Viking records: regional storms to tau 1.2-3.6 superposed (Lemmon et al. 2015; TM-102299 VL1 record max 3.25-3.6 in 1977 storms)
parameters.notes: Ls 180-360; regional storms (days-weeks) add +1 to +2.5.

parameters.name: Peak dust optical depth during a global (planet-encircling) dust storm
parameters.source: 2018/MY34 GDS: MSL Mastcam tau=8.5 (Guzewich et al. 2019 GRL); Opportunity tau>10.7 lower limit (NTRS 20190027303)
parameters.notes: Site-dependent; A&F f-tables end at tau=6, extrapolate beyond.

parameters.name: Global dust storm occurrence rate
parameters.source: 3 GDS in MY25/28/34 over ~10 Mars years (NTRS 20190027303); historical ~1 per 3 Mars years, aperiodic with gaps up to 6 MY
parameters.notes: All observed GDS initiate at Ls 185-310 deg; model as Bernoulli per Mars year.

parameters.name: Global dust storm optical-depth exponential decay time
parameters.source: MSL MY34 record: exponential decay, e-folding 43 sols (NTRS 20190027303, citing Guzewich et al. 2019)
parameters.notes: Rise time ~1-2 weeks; total enhanced-tau duration ~100 sols.

parameters.name: Design-case dust storm duration at tau>=5 (NASA HEOMD sizing case)
parameters.source: Rucker et al., Solar vs. Fission Surface Power for Mars (NTRS 20160002628): 120-day storm at average optical depth 5
parameters.notes: Conservative envelope vs observed ~100 sols enhanced tau in 2018.

parameters.name: Solar array dust deposition (obscuration) rate, horizontal array, between cleanings
parameters.source: Pathfinder 0.28%/sol first 30 sols (NASA/TM-2004-213367); InSight 0.2%/sol observed, 0.28%/sol steady-state model (Lorenz et al. 2020, doi:10.1029/2019EA000992); MER long-term ~0.14%/sol; envelope 0.05-2%/sol (Lorenz et al. 2021 P&SS 105337)
parameters.notes: x3-10 during storm fallout; ~x0.1 for near-vertical panels (Landis used 0.028%/sol).

parameters.name: Dust factor restored by a major natural cleaning event
parameters.source: MER flight history: 2014 Opportunity events boosted power ~70%, arrays imaged near-clean; frequency is site-specific - InSight saw no significant cleaning in 4 years (Lorenz et al. 2021)
parameters.notes: Model as Poisson 0-6 events/Mars-year (site wind climate); do not rely on for design - assume active cleaning restores 0.95-1.0.

parameters.name: Flight III-V triple-junction cell efficiency, BOL, AM0 28 degC
parameters.source: Spectrolab XTJ Prime datasheet 7/2016 (company claim, flight product): 30.7% production average; SolAero ZTJ 29.5%; MER-era 3J ~27.5%; Mars-spectrum-adapted ~32% (Landis NTRS 20070010752)
parameters.notes: Radiation degradation negligible under Mars atmosphere (~20 g/cm2 shielding).

parameters.name: Relative maximum-power temperature coefficient, 3J cell
parameters.source: Computed from Spectrolab XTJ Prime datasheet coefficients (dVmp/dT=-6.5 mV/degC on 2.390 V; dJmp/dT=+8.9 uA/cm2/degC on 17.4 mA/cm2)
parameters.notes: Mars arrays run cold (-50 to +10 degC daytime): +8 to +15% relative efficiency vs 28 degC rating.

parameters.name: Efficiency multiplier for red-shifted dusty-sky spectrum (3J cells, tau~1)
parameters.source: Landis & Hyatt, The Solar Spectrum on the Martian Surface and its Effect on Photovoltaic Performance (NTRS 20070010752): dust absorbs blue, top junction becomes current-limiting
parameters.notes: Worsens with tau; ~1.0 for Si or spectrum-matched cells.

parameters.name: Mars surface array areal mass, system level (blanket + structure + deployment + electrical)
parameters.source: Compact Telescoping Surface Array study (NTRS 20190000437): 1500 kg per 1000 m2 goal, blanket 0.5-1.0 kg/m2; Rucker 2016 UltraFlex-based point ~2.9 kg/m2
parameters.notes: CTSA is a TRL 4-5 design goal; UltraFlex value has flight-adjacent pedigree.

parameters.name: Array specific power at Mars surface noon, clear sky, equator
parameters.source: CTSA study (NTRS 20190000437): 50-80 kW daytime from ~1500 kg, 1000 m2 (200 W/kg at 1 AU equivalent); UltraFlex flight wings 100-150 W/kg at 1 AU
parameters.notes: Equivalent ~19-30 kg per noon-peak kW, generation hardware only (no storage).

parameters.name: Complete solar power system mass per kW continuous (day+night) load
parameters.source: Rucker AIAA Space 2016 (NTRS 20160011275): 11,713 kg Jezero system for ~35 kW day / 26.8 kW night (12,679 kg Columbus); storm-hardened variant 9,800 kg for 22 kW keep-alive (NTRS 20160002628). Fission comparison: 4x10 kWe Kilopower = 9,154 kg (~230 kg/kW)
parameters.notes: Includes generation + Li-ion storage + structure + thermal, excludes lander-to-lander PMAD; equatorial site, modest storm requirement.

parameters.name: Li-ion battery specific energy, pack/system level, Mars-surface rated
parameters.source: JPL D-101146 Energy Storage Technologies report (2017): flight state-of-practice packs <100 Wh/kg (Yardney MER/Phoenix/MSL heritage); development targets 150-250 Wh/kg; modern cells 240-270 Wh/kg imply near-term packs 150-180
parameters.notes: Settlement-era value is a projection; flight-qualified today is the low end.

parameters.name: Li-ion battery round-trip energy efficiency (system level, incl. converters)
parameters.source: Estimate: Li-ion cell-level 94-97% from terrestrial literature; Tesla Powerwall 92.5% DC round-trip (company claim); system studies 84-96%
parameters.notes: Degrades at low temperature and high C-rate.

parameters.name: Allowable depth of discharge for multi-Mars-year cycle life
parameters.source: Estimate: Li-ion cycle-life curves for >2000 cycles (3+ Mars years of daily cycling); JPL D-101146 mission requirements cite 100% DOD only for short missions
parameters.notes: Sizing: battery kWh = night load x 12.3 h / (DOD x round-trip eff).

parameters.name: Regenerative H2/O2 fuel cell specific energy for multi-sol (storm) storage
parameters.source: JPL D-101146 human-lander target >500 Wh/kg; CTSA study baselines 10 kW RFC night power; no flight heritage
parameters.notes: Round-trip efficiency only ~40-55%; beats Li-ion on mass for >30-50 h of storage.

parameters.name: Daily global insolation, horizontal surface, equator, clear sky (tau 0.4)
parameters.source: Integrated from A&F TM-103623 f-table (this work, 4.05 at equinox); matches TM-103623 Table V VL1 max 4.18 kWh/m2-sol at Ls 140
parameters.notes: Range covers aphelion/perihelion Gob swing at low latitude.

parameters.name: Daily global insolation, horizontal, equator, global-storm design tau=5
parameters.source: Integrated from A&F TM-103623 f-table (this work); consistent with Lemmon et al. 2015 flight observation that tau>4 cuts surface insolation only by factor 2-3 due to forward scattering
parameters.notes: At tau 8.5 (GDS peak) extrapolated ~0.55-0.7 kWh/m2/sol; NOT e^-tau scaling.

parameters.name: Worst-sol daily insolation at 48 deg N (winter + Viking-year dust storm)
parameters.source: NASA TM-103623 Table V, VL2 (47.7 N) minimum 315 Wh/m2-day at Ls 280; VL1 (22.3 N) minimum 935 Wh/m2-day for comparison
parameters.notes: Latitude penalty: worst-sol/best-sol = 7.5% at 48N vs 22% at 22N. NASA crewed solar studies restrict to |lat|<~30 deg.

parameters.name: Equivalent peak-sun hours per sol (daily energy / noon power), equator, horizontal
parameters.source: Computed from A&F model (this work): 7.44 h at tau 0.4, falling to 6.4 h at tau 5-6 (profile flattens as diffuse dominates)
parameters.notes: Distillation: kWh/sol per installed kW (rated at 590 W/m2 reference) = 6.9 (tau 0.4), 5.7 (tau 1), 3.3 (tau 3), 2.0 (tau 5); multiply by dust factor and PMAD efficiency.

modeling_notes: L0 (scalar): E_sol[kWh/sol] = P_installed[kW] x EPH x DF x eta_sys, with EPH ~5.5 kWh/sol per installed kW (equator, climatological dust, kW rated at eta*A*590 W/m2), DF~0.85 maintained arrays, eta_sys~0.90 PMAD; storm risk = output x0.3 for ~100 sols with p~0.3 per Mars year. L1 (daily/seasonal): Gob(Ls)=590[1+e cos(Ls-251)]^2/(1-e^2)^2; declination sin(d)=sin(25.19)sin(Ls); daily TOA insolation Hobh=(24/pi)Gob[(pi*wss/180)sin(phi)sin(d)+cos(phi)cos(d)sin(wss)] with wss=acos(-tan phi tan d); surface H = Hobh x T(tau) with T = {0.86@0.4, 0.71@1, 0.41@3, 0.26@5, 0.20@6}; tau(Ls) piecewise 0.45 clear/0.9 dusty seasons + stochastic regional storms + GDS process (Bernoulli 0.3/MY, onset Ls 185-310, peak tau N(9,1), exp decay 43 sols); dust factor DF ODE: -0.28%/sol deposition, Poisson cleaning (site param 0-6/MY, restore to 0.9) or scheduled active cleaning. L2 (sub-sol): cos z = sin phi sin d + cos phi cos d cos w (w=15T-180); beam Gbh=Gob cos z exp(-tau/cos z); global Gh=Gob cos z f(z,tau,al)/(1-al) with bilinear interpolation of the TM-103623 f-table (albedo 0.1; extrapolate log-linearly for tau>6); diffuse=Gh-Gbh; tilted plane via beam AOI + isotropic diffuse; cell temp from energy balance (alpha=0.88, eps=0.85, thin CO2 convection) with eta(T)=eta_ref[1-0.0022(T-301K)] and spectral factor ~0.95; battery SOC integration (round-trip 0.92, DOD 0.7). Validation targets: VL1/VL2 daily insolation tables (TM-103623 Table V), InSight 4.6->0.5 kWh/sol power history, MSL 97% UV drop at MY34 GDS peak. Key trap: storm output scales with the net-flux function (factor ~3 loss at tau 5), NOT exp(-tau) (factor ~150); diffuse light keeps solar viable through storms at reduced power.
key_findings: Mars TOA solar flux averages 586-590 W/m2 (43% of Earth's) and swings 493-718 W/m2 over the eccentric orbit (+/-19%); perihelion boost coincides with the dust-storm season, so the two largest seasonal effects partially cancel at low-latitude sites.

The Appelbaum & Flood model (NASA TM-102299/103623) remains the engineering standard: direct beam = Gob cos z exp(-tau/cos z); global horizontal = Gob cos z f(z,tau,albedo)/(1-albedo) using Pollack's tabulated normalized net flux function - the full f(z,tau) lookup table (tau 0.1-6, z 0-85 deg) was extracted from TM-103623 and is reproduced in the markdown.

Because Mars dust is strongly forward-scattering, global surface insolation falls only x2-3 during a tau=5 global storm (4.0 -> 1.2 kWh/m2/sol at the equator), not by exp(-tau); solar power survives storms at ~25-30% output, which flight data from MER confirmed.

Optical depth climatology from 20+ years of flight data: clear season 0.3-0.6, dusty season background 0.7-1.2 with regional storms to ~3, global storms peaking at tau 8.5-10.8 (2018/MY34), occurring ~1 per 3 Mars years (aperiodic, gaps up to 6 MY), rising in ~2 weeks and decaying with a 43-sol e-folding time; NASA sizes systems to a 120-sol tau=5 design storm.

Dust deposition degrades horizontal arrays ~0.28%/sol (Pathfinder, InSight; MER ~0.14%/sol; envelope 0.05-2%/sol); natural wind cleaning events fully restored MER arrays repeatedly but never occurred at InSight in 4 years - cleaning is a site-specific random process, so settlement designs must assume active dust removal.

Flight 3J cells deliver ~30% AM0 efficiency (XTJ Prime 30.7% datasheet) with a -0.22%/degC relative power coefficient; cold Mars operating temperatures add ~+10% relative, partially offset by a ~5% red-spectrum penalty under dust.

Mars-surface array system mass: ~1.5 kg/m2 (NASA CTSA 1000 m2 study goal; UltraFlex-derived point 2.9 kg/m2), i.e. ~19-30 kg per noon-peak kW; complete solar+storage systems for continuous crewed loads run ~300-450 kg/kW versus ~230 kg/kW for Kilopower fission (Rucker 2016) - solar wins or ties only at low latitudes with modest storm keep-alive requirements.

Energy storage: flight-qualified Li-ion packs are <100 Wh/kg (JPL D-101146) with 130-180 Wh/kg a reasonable settlement-era pack projection, ~92% round-trip; nights are ~12.3 h at the equator; multi-sol storm ride-through favors regenerative fuel cells (~400 Wh/kg, ~50% round-trip, no flight heritage) or array oversizing.

Distilled scalar for trade studies: a maintained equatorial horizontal array yields ~6.9 kWh/sol per installed kW in the clear season, ~5.5 annual average with climatological dust, ~2.0 during a tau=5 storm, and ~1.0 at a tau=8.5 GDS peak (installed kW rated at 590 W/m2 reference irradiance).
Latitude: 48 deg N worst-sol insolation is 0.32 kWh/m2/sol (7.5% of its best sol) vs 0.94 (22%) at 22 deg N - the winter+storm compound drives NASA solar architectures to |latitude| < ~30 deg.
